candidate_problems.text: Can alignment methods be made genuinely theory-driven and capability-robust, rather than dependent on empirical deployment feedback? The conjecture hinges on a largely unresolved distinction between alignment as advance derivation of generally valid constraints versus alignment as iterative outer-loop engineering. The key open question is what kinds of alignment knowledge can transfer across large capability jumps, and how to tell in advance whether a proposed method will remain valid when systems acquire qualitatively new competencies.

What is the actual structure of capability progress: many loosely additive improvements or a few tightly coupled bottlenecks that unlock multiple capacities at once? This is unresolved because the conjecture’s predictive force depends on a structural model of AI progress that we do not yet have. The important problem is to identify whether there exist load-bearing thresholds—such as planning, autonomous research, or training regimes—that would simultaneously advance many competencies and thereby break the feedback assumptions of current safety and governance approaches.

How can governance and evaluation be redesigned for weak-feedback or no-warning-shot regimes? If discontinuity is dangerous mainly because it removes error-correcting loops, then a major open problem is whether institutions can function under one-shot design conditions rather than gradual adaptation. This raises unresolved questions about what monitoring, auditing, access controls, pre-deployment standards, or coordination mechanisms could remain effective when frontier deployment experience is unavailable or misleading.
